Study B · Fluctuating LBM nucleation rate

Fluctuating Shan–Chen LBM and the nucleation rate

Lulli et al. demonstrated that vapour bubbles nucleate spontaneously from a metastable liquid, and measured the steady-state homogeneous nucleation rate \(J\). This is the demonstration Study B builds on directly.

FLBM algorithm

The state of the fluid is the set of populations \(f_i(\mathbf{x},t)\) — the amount of fluid at each site \(\mathbf{x}\) moving along each lattice direction \(\mathbf{c}_i\). Each timestep is one update: a local collision followed by streaming to the neighbouring sites. The collision has three parts — BGK relaxation toward equilibrium, the Shan–Chen force that produces the liquid–vapour coexistence, and thermal noise. The noise is what lets bubbles appear on their own.

One FLBM timestep
Metastable liquid — populations \(f_i(\mathbf{x},t)\)
↓
Collision: \(\;\omega^{\mathrm{BGK}}\Omega^{\mathrm{BGK}}(f_i) + S_i + \eta_i\)
BGK relaxation \(\Omega^{\mathrm{BGK}}\) Shan–Chen force \(S_i\) from the forcing stencil FDT thermal noise \(\eta_i\)
Forcing stencil = the neighbour vectors \(\mathbf{c}_i\) and weights \(w_i\) building the Shan–Chen force \(\;\mathbf{F}^{\mathrm{SC}}(\mathbf{x}) = -G\,\psi(\mathbf{x})\sum_i w_i\,\psi(\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{c}_i\Delta t)\,\mathbf{c}_i\). The weights \(w_i\) set the isotropy and — via the lattice pressure tensor — the surface tension and Tolman length, so the stencil is the knob this paper tunes. Pseudopotential \(\psi(n)=\big(n/(\varepsilon+n)\big)^{1/\varepsilon}\) fixes the non-ideal EOS.
↓
Streaming: \(f_i(\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{c}_i\Delta t,\,t+\Delta t)\)
↓
↺ repeat — noise spontaneously nucleates vapour bubbles
Build binary vapour mask → count bubbles \(N_b(t)\)
↓
Nucleation rate \(J=\dfrac{1}{L^2}\,\dfrac{dN_b}{dt}\Big|_{\text{linear}}\)
Simulation setup and parameters
System
2D square, fully periodic, \(L = 4096\) lattice sites
Mass conservation
exact — so the box evolves through the full nucleation → coalescence sequence
Reduced temperature
\(\Delta\hat T = 0.056\) (close to the critical point)
Noise temperature
\(k_BT = 5\times10^{-5}\) (lu)
Metastable initial state
homogeneous density \(n_{\mathrm{init}}(a)=n_{s,l}(1-a)+a\,n_l\), \(a\approx0.02\), with \(n_l\approx1.264\) (equilibrium liquid) and \(n_{s,l}\approx1.026\) (liquid spinodal)
Tolman values
\(\Delta\delta = 0\) and \(0.5\), reference \(\hat\delta_0\approx0.307\), \(\delta(\Delta\delta)\approx10.97(1+\Delta\delta)\) (lu)
Statistics
\(N_{\mathrm{rep}} = 32\) independent runs, averaged
Framework
GPU-accelerated idea.deploy
The four stages of the dynamics
  1. Inception — a few embryos nucleate from the metastable liquid.
  2. Steady state — independent nucleation events at a constant rate; each new bubble carries a lighter-density halo (a sound wave), and the halos rarely overlap — the events are statistically independent.
  3. Interaction / maximum — halos begin to overlap and the bubble count reaches its maximum.
  4. Coalescence — the largest bubbles merge and the count falls; the fixed-mass box inverts toward a vapour-dominated state.
How the rate is measured
  • Bubble counting — a binary mask marks a site as vapour when the local density is below the equilibrium liquid value by more than \(\tfrac{3}{4}(n_l - n_g)\); connected vapour regions are counted with a standard clustering algorithm (scipy).
  • Rate — \(N_b(t)\) rises from zero, and the nucleation rate is the slope of the linear steady-state region, normalised by the system area:
$$ J = \frac{1}{L^2}\left.\frac{dN_b}{dt}\right|_{\text{linear}}. $$

Measured values: \(J\approx3.26\times10^{-9}\) (lu) at \(\Delta\delta=0\) and \(3.61\times10^{-9}\) (lu) at \(\Delta\delta=0.5\).

Flat-interface surface tension vs. the Tolman length

The central idea of the paper is that the forcing-stencil weights carry more degrees of freedom than a single surface-tension knob. Working through the lattice pressure tensor, the surface tension is expanded in orders of the interface-profile derivatives, giving separate, tunable coefficients:

$$ \hat\sigma_0 = -\frac{e_4}{2}\ \text{(leading, flat interface)}, \qquad \hat\sigma_1 = \frac{e_6}{4}\ \text{(higher order)}, \qquad \hat\delta_0 = \tfrac{1}{12}+\tfrac{95e_4}{432}+\tfrac{7e_6}{72}-\tfrac{5e_8}{144}\ \text{(Tolman)}. $$
  • Flat-interface surface tension \(\sigma_0\) is set at leading order by \(e_4\) (i.e. \(\hat\sigma_0\)); tuning it this way, however, also changes the equilibrium interface profile and bulk densities.
  • Higher-order surface tension via \(\hat\sigma_1\) (the coefficient \(e_6\)) instead leaves the flat-interface equilibrium densities essentially unchanged (variations \(\sim10^{-2}\)).
  • Tolman length \(\delta\) is then tuned by \(e_8\) alone, holding \(\hat\sigma_0\) and \(\hat\sigma_1\) — and therefore \(\sigma_0\), the bulk densities, and the flat-interface profile — fixed. Bulk densities stay constant to \(\mathcal{O}(10^{-13})\), and the flat profile is unchanged across a wide range of \(\Delta\delta\).

The consequence is a clean separation: the flat interface (\(\sigma_0\), profile, densities) is a property of \(e_4,e_6\), while the interface curvature correction — the Tolman length that shifts the nucleation barrier — is a separate property of \(e_8\). On curved interfaces (droplets and bubbles) the Laplace law is recovered at a fixed slope independent of \(\Delta\delta\), and the sub-leading "fanning" of \(\Delta P\cdot R_s/\sigma_0\) with \(1/R_s\) is exactly the Tolman signature. This is the first time \(\delta\) has been controlled independently of \(\sigma_0\) in a Shan–Chen model.

How spurious currents are handled

Spurious (parasitic) currents are small non-physical velocities that appear near a curved interface even when the fluid should be at rest. In the Shan–Chen model they come from the forcing stencil: the discrete sum that builds the force cannot balance the pressure gradient exactly at a curved interface, and the residual imbalance drives a persistent flow. Because that imbalance follows the symmetry of the lattice, the fix is to make the stencil more isotropic. Lulli et al. use two levers, both living in the same forcing stencil:

  • Higher isotropy order. The historical route (Refs. 64–65): add more vector symmetry groups so the forcing stencil satisfies higher-order isotropy conditions. This decays the spurious currents fastest, but the stencil grows rapidly — the 12th-order isotropic stencil needs \(N_e=56\) vectors in 2D and \(284\) in 3D, i.e. many more memory reads.
  • Raising \(e_4\) at fixed stencil size. The interface width is tied to the spurious-current intensity; increasing \(e_4\) (thicker interface, larger \(\varepsilon\)) gives a roughly exponential reduction of the maximum spurious Mach number \(u_M\) — while keeping the vector count constant (\(N_e=16\) in 2D). This alone cuts the currents by about an order of magnitude.

The two mechanisms are complementary: a higher-isotropy stencil decays them faster but costs vectors; tuning \(e_4\) buys most of the reduction for free. For the production stencils the authors provide a sixth-order isotropic set that reduces spurious currents at only \(N_e=16\) (2D) / \(44\) (3D) — a factor 3.5–6.4 fewer memory reads than the maximally-isotropic 12th-order stencil for a comparable result.

Why this matters for Study B
The nucleation rate is exponentially sensitive to the free-energy barrier, so a spurious velocity field near forming embryos would bias \(J\). Study B inherits exactly this stencil machinery: it needs the spurious currents suppressed and the surface tension and Tolman length controlled at the same time — which is only possible because both are expressed through the same tunable forcing-stencil weights.
The Tolman-length result
Increasing the Tolman length \(\delta\) at fixed flat-interface surface tension \(\sigma_0\) lowers the free-energy barrier \(\Delta F\approx-4\pi R_c^2\,\sigma(R_s)\) with \(\sigma(R_s)\approx\sigma_0(1-\delta/R_s)\), so \(J\propto\exp(-\Delta F/k_BT)\) rises. The measured rate changes by roughly 10% between \(\Delta\delta=0\) and \(0.5\) — the first demonstration that a multiphase LBM can resolve nucleation physics beyond the classical CNT approximation.
Why it is our starting point
Study B extends exactly this 2D fluctuating Shan–Chen demonstration to 3D, imposes the MBWR Lennard–Jones EOS via the multipseudopotential construction, validates the noise amplitude in physical units through \(S(k\!\to\!0)\), and converts the extracted \(J\) to SI for an order-of-magnitude benchmark against MD. The \(\sim\)10% Tolman sensitivity shown here is why Study B must control the Tolman length, not just the surface tension.

Source: M. Lulli and E. S. C. Ching, "Higher-order tuning of interface physics in multiphase lattice Boltzmann models," Phys. Rev. E 112, 035308 (2025), Sec. V D and Figs. 9–10. Fluctuating-hydrodynamics SC implementation: their Ref. 44. Animation frames provided by the author.

Study B · Fluctuating LBM nucleation rate — companion to the candidacy presentation. Source: Lulli and Ching, Phys. Rev. E 112, 035308 (2025). Equations via MathJax.
